visits.text: Gisenyi — officially Rubavu today — is Rwanda's border town with the Congo, sitting on the northern shore of Lake Kivu. This 2,700 km² lake is one of Africa's most overlooked great lakes, which is a shame: no crocodiles or hippos, clear water, sandy beaches and volcanoes on the horizon. In 2002, lava from Nyiragongo flowed into the lake on the Congolese side — which accounts for the stretches of black volcanic sand you'll notice along the shore. The Bralirwa brewery — Rwanda's only brewery — has been operating in town since the 1950s.

Rubavu public beach, Rwanda's only public beach, runs for a few hundred metres along the waterfront. The lake water is clear and safe — swimming is popular and risk-free here. The Avenue de la Coopération promenade follows the lakeshore toward the Congolese border under a canopy of palms and flamboyant trees.

activities.text: Sunset dugout canoe trip on Lake Kivu to the nearby islands. Napoleon Island is home to a colony of straw-coloured fruit bats that take to the air en masse at dusk — an unexpectedly spectacular natural display. Local canoes depart from the main pier.

days_plan.itin_title: East Africa Safari: Kenya, Uganda, Rwanda & Tanzania

visits.text: The Kigali Genocide Memorial, inaugurated in April 2004 on the tenth anniversary, is the resting place of 250,000 victims murdered in Kigali in 1994. It was inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List in 2023. The visit opens with a chronological exhibition — Belgian colonisation, 1980s propaganda, the hundred days from April to July 1994 — before building in intensity to the children's room, with photographs and individual stories of each child killed. It is difficult. Take your time. The audio guide is strongly recommended.

Kimironko Market is the largest covered market in Kigali — a maze of kitenge fabric stalls, imigongo geometric art (made from cow dung), vegetables and music sellers. The atmosphere is lively and largely untouched by tourism. Haggling is expected.

Rwanda–Uganda border crossing. The Gatuna (Rwanda) / Katuna (Uganda) post is 90 minutes north of Kigali on the Kabale road. It's the main road crossing between the two countries — the best equipped and the fastest. From Kabale, the drive to Bwindi is a further 2–3 hours depending on which sector you're heading to — Buhoma or Nkuringo — largely on dirt tracks.

Start with the hardest part: the Kigali Genocide Memorial retraces the events of 1994 with a rigor and dignity that leaves you speechless. More than 250,000 victims rest here. Allow at least two hours, and don't plan anything lighthearted immediately after. Entry is free, donations welcome.

Change of pace at Kimironko Market, the city's largest. Wax fabrics stacked to the ceiling, tropical fruit, bulk spices—this is where Kigalians do their shopping. Haggle over prices, especially on textiles. Go in the morning to avoid the crush.

The Kandt House, former residence of Rwanda's first German colonial governor (1908), has become the country's Natural History Museum. Small but well done, it covers the geology, fauna, and flora of Rwanda. Entry: 6,000 Rwandan francs.

The Nyamirambo district pulses with an energy you won't find anywhere else in Kigali. Join the guided tour from the Nyamirambo Women's Center: local markets, sewing workshops, street food. Allow 2 hours and 15,000 Rwandan francs—the money funds projects for women in the neighborhood.

The Inema Arts Center, founded by brothers Emmanuel and Innocent Nkuranga, exhibits contemporary Rwandan art in an open garden setting. Dance and painting workshops happen weekly. Free entry, café on-site.

activities.text: Kigali is Africa's cleanest capital — plastic bags have been banned since 2008, and the last Saturday of every month is a national cleaning day called Umuganda in which everyone participates. The Nyamirambo neighbourhood, a historically Muslim working-class quarter, is worth an hour on foot for its restaurants, coffee shops and genuine local energy. Dinner at one of East Africa's best restaurants before hitting the road again tomorrow.
Climb Mount Kigali on foot for a panorama over the city and its thousand hills. The trail starts from the Kiyovu district; allow 1.5 hours round trip. Late afternoon light is ideal for photos.

days_plan.itin_title: East Africa Safari: Kenya, Uganda, Rwanda & Tanzania
Rwanda: 13-Day Loop Through Gorillas, Lakes & Volcanoes

places.name: Kinigi

visits.text: Volcanoes National Park shelters five of the eight Virunga volcanoes: Karisimbi, Bisoke, Muhabura, Gahinga and Sabyinyo. Tucked into Rwanda's northwest, 2.5 hours from Kigali, these bamboo-forested, mist-shrouded summits form one of the last refuges of the mountain gorilla. The current global population stands at roughly 1,000 individuals — 604 of them in the Virunga massif — and is slowly growing thanks to sustained conservation efforts.

The Karisoke research site, founded by Dian Fossey in 1967, is accessible by hiking trail from the Kinigi headquarters. Fossey spent 18 years shielding gorillas from poachers before being murdered in her cabin in 1985 — a crime that has never been officially solved. Her grave is on-site, surrounded by the gorillas she fought hardest to protect. The hike is physically demanding (roughly 3 hours return), but the historical weight of the place is extraordinary.

Gorilla trekking in Volcanoes National Park: you end up a few metres from a silverback — a dominant male that can weigh over 200 kg — watching you with an unnerving calm. Mountain gorillas share 98% of our DNA. Each family is visited by a maximum of 8 people for one hour per day only. The trek itself can last anywhere from 30 minutes to 6 hours depending on where the family has roamed that morning — bring gaiters, a rain jacket and solid hiking boots.

activities.text: Golden monkey trekking. If your gorilla permit is for tomorrow, start today with the golden monkeys — less famous, but just as extraordinary. These primates, endemic to the Albertine Rift (Cercopithecus mitis kandti), crash through the bamboo in noisy troops of up to 80. Five habituated groups live in the park; the trek lasts two to four hours depending on where the troop has wandered that day.

Option: climb Mount Bisoke (3,711 m). If you have a second free day in the park, Bisoke offers a 6-hour return hike to a summit crater lake. The vegetation shifts every hour — bamboo forest, then Hagenia woodland, then alpine moorland. On a clear day the view stretches across the Virunga range all the way into the DRC.

days_plan.itin_title: East Africa Safari: Kenya, Uganda, Rwanda & Tanzania
East Africa Safari: Kenya, Uganda, Rwanda & Tanzania

map_keywords: Dian Fossey Karisoke Research Centre Rwanda
Gorilla Trekking Rwanda Volcanoes National Park

visits.text: Nyungwe is one of the largest montane forests in central Africa: 1,000 km² of unbroken primary forest that has stood for millennia. It supplies 75% of Rwanda's water resources — roughly half the rivers drain toward the Nile, the other half toward the Congo. The park harbours 13 primate species, 300 bird species (26 of them endemic to the Albertine Rift) and over 1,000 tree species. The Uwinka visitor centre, halfway along the Butare–Cyangugu road, is the departure point for all activities.

If you've never set foot in an ancient rainforest, Nyungwe will be a shock. This remnant of the great forest that once covered Africa spans 970 km², shelters 13 primate species, 300 bird species, and over 1,000 plant species—unmatched in East Africa.

Nyungwe's canopy walkway has you walking 70 meters above the ground, across 160 meters. Black-and-white colobus monkeys sometimes pass beneath your feet. Allow 2 hours including the approach walk. Rate: USD 40 for foreigners.

activities.text: The Canopy Walk is the highest suspended walkway in the region: 160 metres long, 70 metres above the forest floor, built in 2010 by USAID. You reach it via the 2 km Igishigishigi trail — not the moment to discover you have a fear of heights. From up there, the forest stretches unbroken to the Congo horizon. Black-and-white colobus monkeys sometimes leap between trees at eye level.

Option: chimpanzee trekking in the Cyamudongo forest patch. This 19 km² fragment of forest tucked between tea plantations is home to a well-habituated group of 30 chimpanzees. The trek is 4 hours return from the Gisakura reception. Nyungwe's chimps are more mobile than those in Kibale, Uganda — sightings are less guaranteed, but tracking them through tea-covered hills is an experience in its own right.

The chimpanzee trek starts at dawn at the park HQ in Uwinka. Nyungwe's 500 chimpanzees live in groups, about thirty of which are habituated to human presence. One hour of observation once the group is found. Permit: USD 100/person. Minimum age: 15.
Descend to Isumo Falls via the forest trail (3 hours round trip). The path crosses troops of colobus and L'Hoest's monkeys. A park guide is mandatory.

Take a detour to the Gisakura tea plantation at the park's southern entrance. The fluorescent-green hills of tea bushes are photogenic at any hour. Some plantations offer a guided tour of the production process.

days_plan.itin_title: East Africa Safari: Kenya, Uganda, Rwanda & Tanzania
Rwanda: 13-Day Loop Through Gorillas, Lakes & Volcanoes

visits.text: You don't expect a savannah safari in Rwanda—and yet, Akagera National Park is right here, the country's only park of its kind. Decimated by poaching after 1994, it has seen the return of lions (2015), black rhinos (2017), and white rhinos (2021) thanks to African Parks. Today you can spot the Big Five across 1,122 km².

The northern sector of the park is richest in wildlife: elephants, giraffes, zebras, buffalo, antelope. The track winds between lakes and savannah, with views over the Tanzanian plains in the distance. Leave at sunrise to maximize sightings—animals hide by midday.

activities.text: Take the cruise on Lake Ihema, Rwanda's second-largest lake. Hippos, crocodiles, fish eagles, elephants coming to drink—in two hours on the water, the parade is constant. Departures scheduled at 8 AM and 3 PM; book at your lodge.
If you're staying two nights, try the night game drive in the northern sector. Leopards, hyenas, genets, and bushbabies appear after sunset. Park vehicle required, limited spaces.

days_plan.itin_title: Rwanda: 13-Day Loop Through Gorillas, Lakes & Volcanoes

visits.text: Gisenyi (Rubavu) is Rwanda's beach resort—sandy beaches, palm trees, turquoise water of Lake Kivu. Across the border on foot is Goma in the DRC. Relaxed vibe, lakeside cafés, Rwandan families on weekends.

activities.text: Put on a swimsuit and dive into Lake Kivu from Gisenyi's public beach. The water is fresh, clean, without parasites or dangerous animals. Rwandan children dive off the pier with contagious energy.

Take a bike and ride along the northern shore of the lake on the road toward Kigali. Tea-covered hills, fishing villages, views of the Virunga volcanoes to the north. It's the start of the Congo Nile Trail, 227 km of coastal path all the way to Rusizi.

days_plan.itin_title: Rwanda: 13-Day Loop Through Gorillas, Lakes & Volcanoes

visits.text: Seven rooms, no photos, and a cathedral silence: the Ethnographic Museum in Huye (formerly Butare) is the most comprehensive in the country. It traces pre-colonial Rwandan life: clothing, hunting tools, pottery, musical instruments. Built in 1989 with Belgian support, its roof evokes traditional drums. Entry: 6,000 Rwandan francs.

Huye itself is Rwanda's university town, calmer and more scholarly than Kigali. Belgian colonial architecture still marks the city center. Stop at a local café to taste Rwandan coffee—the country produces a highly regarded high-altitude arabica.

activities.text: Visit a cooperative of agaseke weavers—the traditional Rwandan woven basket, inscribed on the intangible heritage list. Some cooperatives around Huye welcome visitors for demonstrations. Plan on 10,000 Rwandan francs for a handmade basket.

days_plan.itin_title: Rwanda: 13-Day Loop Through Gorillas, Lakes & Volcanoes

visits.text: Kibuye (renamed Karongi) is the prettiest town on Lake Kivu—pines, eucalyptus, green hills, and a handful of volcanic islands scattered across the water. Sunsets facing west are among the finest in Rwanda.

The Kibuye genocide memorial church, on a hill above the lake, is a place of contemplation. Its colored stained-glass windows contrast with the tragedy that unfolded here in 1994. A moment of silence is called for.

activities.text: Go out with the night fishermen on Lake Kivu. Every evening, dozens of pirogues leave on whistle to fish for sambaza (tiny silver fish) by lamplight. A unique sonic and visual experience—allow 3 hours.
Rent a kayak and explore the islands around Kibuye at your own pace. No crocodiles, no bilharzia—Lake Kivu is one of the few large African lakes where swimming is safe.

days_plan.itin_title: Rwanda: 13-Day Loop Through Gorillas, Lakes & Volcanoes

visits.text: The entrance is cathedral-sized, darkness is total from the second bend: Musanze Caves stretch 2 km underground, formed by lava flows from Bisoke and Sabyinyo volcanoes. Opened to tourism in 2013, they have 31 entrances. During the 1994 genocide, thousands took refuge here. Helmet and lamp provided.

At the cave exit, local artisans display woven baskets, bows, and jewelry. You can also watch a banana beer (ugwegwe) brewing workshop. The site offers panoramic views of Gahinga, Karisimbi, and Muhabura volcanoes.

days_plan.itin_title: Rwanda: 13-Day Loop Through Gorillas, Lakes & Volcanoes

visits.text: Napoleon Island (Tembabagoyi) owes its nickname to its silhouette resembling Bonaparte's hat. Climb to the summit for a panorama over the archipelago, then descend among thousands of straw-colored fruit bats hanging from the branches. The species is protected in Rwanda. Come late in the day to watch them fly off.

days_plan.itin_title: Rwanda: 13-Day Loop Through Gorillas, Lakes & Volcanoes

visits.text: The Nyamata Church, 30 km south of Kigali, has been left exactly as it was since April 1994. More than 10,000 people had taken refuge there. The victims' clothing still covers the pews. It's one of the most shattering sites in the country. Free entry.

days_plan.itin_title: Rwanda: 13-Day Loop Through Gorillas, Lakes & Volcanoes

visits.text: The Nyamyumba hot springs gush at 70–75°C before flowing into Lake Kivu. Two rudimentary pools made from sandbags let you soak. Locals attribute medicinal properties to these waters. No changing room—come prepared.

days_plan.itin_title: Rwanda: 13-Day Loop Through Gorillas, Lakes & Volcanoes

visits.text: The King's Palace in Nyanza is a giant thatched beehive—literally. This faithful reconstruction of the traditional royal residence replicates the honeycomb-shaped architecture that housed the Rwandan court. The museum, opened in 2008, covers five centuries of monarchy until its abolition in 1961.

Behind the palace, the Inyambo cattle with their enormous horns are descendants of the royal herd. Their keepers still sing them traditional lullabies—a strange and touching spectacle. King Mutara III's Art Deco residence, built by the Belgians in the 1930s, rounds out the visit.

days_plan.itin_title: Rwanda: 13-Day Loop Through Gorillas, Lakes & Volcanoes

visits.text: The twin lakes of Burera and Ruhondo, separated by a strip of land about 1 km wide, occupy craters formed by Mount Sabyinyo's eruptions. The water is deep blue, terraced hills plunge straight into it. It's postcard Rwanda, without a tourist in sight.

activities.text: Take a traditional pirogue on Lake Burera to visit the islands of Bushongo or Munanira. Local fishermen teach you their techniques on the water. Allow USD 20–30 per person for a 2-hour ride.
Walk to a neighboring village for an immersion in rural Rwandan life. Some lodges organize community visits with a shared meal at a local home. It's often the memory that marks travelers most.

days_plan.itin_title: Rwanda: 13-Day Loop Through Gorillas, Lakes & Volcanoes

visits.text: Five volcanoes lined up like sentinels, the tallest at 4,507 m: Volcanoes National Park protects the Rwandan side of the Virunga range. This is where Dian Fossey studied and defended mountain gorillas for 18 years, until her murder in 1985.

The Gorilla Guardians Village (formerly Iby'Iwacu), at the foot of the park, is run by former poachers turned conservationists. Dances, archery, banana beer brewing demonstration—the visit directly funds gorilla protection. Allow 1.5 hours.

activities.text: The gorilla trek is a once-in-a-lifetime experience. Briefing at 7 AM at Kinigi HQ, then forest hike (1 to 6 hours depending on the group assigned) to reach one of the 11 habituated families. One hour face-to-face with the gorillas, just meters away. Emotion guaranteed. Permit: USD 1,500/person; book months in advance.

Hike to Dian Fossey's grave and the ruins of her Karisoke research center. The trail climbs through dense bamboo forest for about 3 hours. The spot, on the volcano flank, is moving and silent. Rate: USD 75/person.

Lighter alternative: the golden monkey trek lasts 2–3 hours in the bamboo forest. These primates, endemic to the Virungas, have bright orange fur and live in groups of around a hundred. Permit: USD 100.

days_plan.itin_title: Rwanda: 13-Day Loop Through Gorillas, Lakes & Volcanoes
